item.title: Effortlessly Calculate 100+ Technical Indicators in Python – A Quick Guide
item.author: Shridhar G Vatharkar
item.content:encoded:
Technical indicators are essential for detailed market analysis and data-driven decision-making. This tutorial will walk you through retrieving historical forex data using the TraderMade API and computing key technical indicators with the Python TA-Lib library. By the end, you will have the necessary tools to integrate these indicators into your applications, platforms, and trading systems.Our API provides real-time data on a wide range of tradable assets, including over 32 US stocks, 10 significant indices, precious metals, and access to more than 100 currencies (explore currencies & CFDs).Before proceeding, it is essential to install the TA-Lib library. Since pip often fails to build this library on specific systems, mainly Windows, we will demonstrate an alternative method using a wheel file.Linux users should refer to the official TA-Lib library. Windows users should visit the TA-Lib Windows wheel page, download the appropriate wheel file based on their Python version and system architecture (32-bit or 64-bit), and ensure that pip is executed from the exact location as the downloaded file.In the example below, we have downloaded a wheel file compatible with 64-bit computers running Python 3.10. Thus, the following download method is recommended:pip install requests pandas pip install TA_Lib-0.4.32-cp310-cp310-win_amd64.whl
After installing the library, register with TraderMade to get your free Forex API key. Next, install TraderMade for seamless integration.
Step 1: Import Required Libraries Start by importing the essential data retrieval, processing, and analysis libraries.import talib import tradermade as tm
Step 2: Configure Your API Key Insert your TraderMade API key to enable authentication.# Replace Your TraderMade API key tm.set_rest_api_key("api_key")
Step 3: Define a Function to Retrieve Market Data Develop a function to fetch historical forex data for a selected currency pair over a specified date range.tm.timeseries(currency='EURUSD', start="2024-08-05",end="2024-11-04",interval="daily",fields=["open", "high", "low","close"])
Step 4: Compute Essential Technical Indicators TA-Lib offers numerous indicators, including candlestick pattern recognition. Below are some key technical indicators widely used by traders:Simple Moving Average (SMA) Smooths price data over a defined period (e.g., 20 days) to identify trends.Relative Strength Index (RSI) A momentum oscillator that measures the speed and change of price movements, helping to detect overbought or oversold conditions.Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD) Analyzes the relationship between two moving averages of a financial instrument, aiding in spotting potential market momentum shifts and generating buy/sell signals.# Calculate technical indicators using TA-Lib df['SMA_20'] = talib.SMA(df['close'], timeperiod=20) # 20-period Simple Moving Average
df['RSI_14'] = talib.RSI(df['close'], timeperiod=14) # 14-period Relative Strength Index df['MACD'], df['MACD_signal'], df['MACD_hist'] = talib.MACD(df['close'], fastperiod=12, slowperiod=26, signalperiod=9) Finally, the last few rows of the DataFrame will be displayed to examine the OHLC data alongside the computed indicators.df = df.set_index("date") df = df.dropna() print(df.head()) open high low close SMA_20 RSI_14 MACD MACD_signal MACD_hist
date 2024-09-19 1.11172 1.11790 1.10686 1.11621 1.109625 62.318384 0.003357 0.003439 -0.000083 2024-09-20 1.11626 1.11820 1.11362 1.11631 1.109472 62.410940 0.003576 0.003467 0.000109 2024-09-23 1.11646 1.11674 1.10834 1.11114 1.109223 54.902623 0.003295 0.003432 -0.000138 2024-09-24 1.11114 1.11811 1.11036 1.11797 1.109199 61.493338 0.003582 0.003462 0.000119 2024-09-25 1.11799 1.12141 1.11217 1.11329 1.109263 55.507279 0.003392 0.003448 -0.000056 Below is the full implementation—review it carefully.# Display the data with indicators
import talib import tradermade as tm
tm.set_rest_api_key("api_key")
df = tm.timeseries(currency='EURUSD', start="2024-08-05",end="2024-11-04",interval="daily",fields=["open", "high", "low","close"])
df['SMA_20'] = talib.SMA(df['close'], timeperiod=20) # 20-period Simple Moving Average
df['RSI_14'] = talib.RSI(df['close'], timeperiod=14) # 14-period Relative Strength Index df['MACD'], df['MACD_signal'], df['MACD_hist'] = talib.MACD(df['close'], fastperiod=12, slowperiod=26, signalperiod=9)
df = df.set_index("date") df = df.dropna() print(df.head()) This tutorial demonstrated how to retrieve historical forex data using the TraderMade SDK and compute key technical indicators such as SMA, RSI, and MACD with the TA-Lib library. However, this is just the beginning—TA-Lib offers over 100 indicators, including candlestick patterns, applicable to various markets.If you found this guide helpful, share our tutorials or reference them in your blogs. Don't hesitate to reach out for any questions or assistance with TA-Lib installation. Happy coding!

item.title: Ultimate Guide to Python's 19 Simple Statements (Definitions, History, Examples)
item.author: Mike Vincent
item.content:encoded:
In Python, a is one complete instruction that tells the computer what to do. Python executes statements sequentially from top to bottom.Python has two statement types: and . Simple statements are single-line instructions like , , and . Compound statements control other statements using a header line followed by indented code blocks, such as , , and .This guide covers all 19 Python simple statements from Python 1.4 (1996) through Python 3.13.2 (2025), including: and statements for running code and storing values statements like , , , , , and with and statements for accessing external code with and like and By the end, you'll understand each simple statement's purpose, evolution, and practical usage. An is a standalone expression on a line by itself, which the Python interpreter evaluates and then (unless it is used interactively). In other words, any expression can be a statement. Commonly, expression statements are used for calling functions or methods (for their side effects) or simply computing a value without storing or printing it. If you use an expression statement in the interactive Python shell, Python will display the result; but in a script, the result is computed and then thrown away if not assigned to a variable or otherwise used. Think of an expression statement as doing a calculation on scratch paper and then immediately tossing the paper away. You got the calculation done (Python evaluates it), but you didn't keep the result. For example, you might punch a number into a calculator just to see it, but if you don't record it or use it, it vanishes. Similarly, writing an expression as a statement computes the value, but unless something is done with it (like assigning it or printing it), it's gone after execution. Expression statements have been part of Python since the very beginning (early 1990s). Python's design has always allowed any expression to stand alone as a statement. In the interactive REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop), the interpreter automatically prints the result of expression statements, which is why you can type and see as output. In regular script execution, however, expression statements produce no output by default. This behavior hasn't fundamentally changed over Python's history, although newer Python versions improved the interactive interpreter to make the output formatting more user-friendly. Overall, the concept of expression statements remains simple and consistent across Python versions.
Example 1: Calling a function as an expression statement In this example, we define a function and then call it as a statement. The function call is an expression that produces a side effect (printing a message). We do not assign its return value (it returns None implicitly), we just use the call on its own line as a statement.
Example 2: Expression statement with no visible effect Here, we use an arithmetic expression as a statement. In a script, this will compute the value but not display or store it. We follow it with a print to show that execution continues, and that the previous expression’s result was effectively unused.(In this script, the expression is evaluated (15) and then discarded, so nothing is output from that line. Only the message is printed.)
2. Assignment Statements ()
An binds a value to a variable name (or multiple names). It uses the operator with a target on the left and an expression on the right. Python evaluates the expression on the right-hand side and that value to the variable(s) on the left-hand side. Assignment creates a reference in a particular scope. You can also assign to multiple variables at once (tuple unpacking) or chain assignments (assigning the same value to several names). Importantly, assignment in Python does produce a value (which is why you cannot embed an assignment in an expression in Python, unlike some languages). It's a statement, not an expression. Assignment is like . Imagine you have a box and you write "Cookies" on it to indicate what’s inside. Similarly, doing in Python labels the name to refer to the value . If you do multiple assignments, it's like putting labels on multiple containers or swapping labels between them. Assignment has been in Python from the very start. Python has always supported assigning values to single variables and to tuple/list structures (e.g. ). Over time, the language introduced more powerful assignment features: for instance, was extended in Python 3 with the "starred" expression (PEP 3132) to capture remaining items, as in . The semantics of basic assignment (simple ) have remained unchanged. Python does multiple assignment by evaluating the right side first and then assigning to each target from left to right. In all versions, trying to assign to an undeclared name creates that name in the current local/global scope (Python uses dynamic name binding), and trying to assign to an expression (like ) is a syntax error. Overall, assignment statements are fundamental and have seen only minor enhancements (like extended unpacking) over Python’s history.
Example 1: Basic and multiple assignment Here we assign a single value to a variable, then use multiple assignment to swap values between two variables. Python allows multiple variables to be assigned in one statement by separating them with commas on the left (and optionally on the right using a tuple of values).
Example 2: Sequence unpacking assignment In this example, a list of three numbers is unpacked into three variables in one assignment statement. The number of variables on the left must match the length of the sequence on the right. This shows how assignment can decompose data structures.
3. Augmented Assignment Statements (e.g. , )
An is a shorthand statement that combines an arithmetic or bitwise operation with assignment. For example, is an augmented assignment that adds 3 to the current value of and assigns the result back to . Python supports augmented operators for addition (), subtraction (), multiplication (), true division (), floor division (), modulus (), exponentiation (), bit shifts (, ), and bitwise AND/OR/XOR (, , ). The effect is equivalent to (for ), but with the potential benefit of performing the operation in-place for mutable types and evaluating the left-hand side only once. Augmented assignment is like a . Imagine you have a counter on a website showing the number of visitors. Instead of writing on a clipboard "new_count = old_count + 1" every time someone visits, you just add 1 in place on the counter. Augmented assignment does that: it updates the value in place. It's conceptually similar to saying "increase this by that amount" in one breath, instead of two separate steps (calculate, then assign). Augmented assignment operators were introduced relatively later in Python’s development. They were added in as part of a proposal to make in-place modifications and updates more convenient (PEP 203 – Augmented Assignments | peps.python.org) (PEP 203 – Augmented Assignments | peps.python.org). Before that, programmers had to write out the full form () every time. The introduction of augmented assignment (PEP 203) brought Python in line with many other languages that had similar syntax. Augmented assignments can offer performance benefits by avoiding a second lookup of the variable and, for mutable objects, potentially modifying in place. The behavior of augmented assignment has stayed consistent since introduction: it first evaluates the target variable (only once) and the right-hand expression, then performs the operation. If the left-hand object type supports an in-place method (like list extending with ), it will use it; otherwise it falls back to the normal operation and assignment. This feature has remained unchanged in Python 3, aside from supporting new types as they come (e.g., works with sequences for concatenation).
Example 1: Numeric augmented assignment This example demonstrates using on a number. We start with a value and then increment it by some amount using augmented assignment. The end result is the original value increased accordingly.
Example 2: String augmented assignment (concatenation) Augmented assignment works on many types. Here we use on a string to concatenate another string to it, and on a list to extend it with another list. These operations modify the original object (in case of the list) or create a new string in a shortened syntax. Hello World [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] (Notice that for the list, is essentially , modifying the list in place. For the string, a new string is created because strings are immutable.)
4. Annotated Assignment Statements (Variable Annotations)
An adds a type hint (annotation) to a variable target as part of the assignment. The syntax is . The part after the colon is a type annotation (which can be any valid type expression). This statement will assign the value to the variable, and store the annotation in the variable’s metadata ( in the containing namespace). Importantly, Python does not enforce the type at runtime – annotations are mostly for static type checkers, documentation, or runtime introspection. You can also use annotated assignment without an actual assignment (e.g., ) to declare a type for a variable that will be assigned later. Annotated assignments do not restrict the variable’s usage; they are purely informational. Think of annotated assignments as labeling a variable with extra information about what type of value it is supposed to hold, similar to writing a note next to a box saying "should contain tools". It doesn't lock the box to only accept tools (someone could still put something else in), but it communicates the intended content. This helps others (and tools) understand what's expected to be in the box (variable). Variable annotations were introduced in via PEP 526 (What’s New In Python 3.6 — Python 3.13.2 documentation). Before that, Python had function annotations (PEP 3107 in Python 3.0) for parameters and return types, but no direct syntax for annotating standalone variables. The addition of annotated assignment allowed programmers to include type hints for module-level and class-level variables and even local variables. Initially, these annotations were mainly for static analysis tools like and for documentation purposes, as Python’s interpreter ignores the annotations at runtime (except for storing them). Over time, the ecosystem (e.g., IDEs, linters, frameworks) has embraced type annotations for better code clarity and error checking. One important change that came later: in Python 3.7 and 3.8, the handling of annotations was optimized (PEP 563 introduced an option to treat them as strings internally to reduce runtime overhead). In Python 3.11+, the evaluation of annotations is back to immediate by default (with the option from __future__ import annotationsto postpone). Throughout, the syntax of annotated assignment remains as . This feature reflects Python’s move towards supporting optional static typing without changing the dynamic nature of the language.
Example 1: Basic annotated assignment In this example, we annotate a variable with a type and assign a value to it. We then print the value and type to show that the annotation does not enforce the type (Python still allows the variable to hold any type). We also demonstrate changing the variable to a different type — Python will not stop us, but a type checker might warn about it.( was annotated as an int, and initially had an int value 25. We then assigned a string to . Python allowed it because the annotation is not enforced at runtime — it’s just metadata. A static type checker, however, would flag that second assignment as incompatible with the declared type.)
Example 2: Accessing annotations metadata This example shows that annotations are stored in a special dictionary. We create a global variable with an annotation and later inspect the dictionary to see the recorded types. We also define a class with annotated class variables to illustrate how annotations can be used in classes. {'data': <class 'float'>} {'x': <class 'int'>, 'y': <class 'int'>} In the output, the first line shows that the global variable is annotated as float. The second line shows the class has annotations for and as ints. These annotations can be used by tools or libraries (for example, dataclass or pydantic) to automatically handle typing logic. Python itself doesn’t use these annotations to enforce types — they’re for the developers and tooling. The is used to into the code. It has the form assert condition, optional_message. When an runs, it evaluates the . If the condition is true (truthy), nothing happens and execution continues. If the condition is false (falsy), Python raises an exception, halting the program (unless the exception is caught). The optional message, if provided, will be used as the error message in the AssertionError. Essentially, serves as a sanity check that if the program is correct. It’s often used to validate internal assumptions, inputs, or invariants in the code during development. Notably, when Python is run in optimized mode (using the flag), all assert statements are skipped (removed), so they have no effect at runtime. Using an is like having a red flag in your code that raises an alarm if something is off. Imagine a door with a sensor that triggers an alarm if the door is opened when it’s not supposed to be. Similarly, an assert checks a condition; if the condition fails (door opens unexpectedly), it (throws an error) to alert you. If everything is as expected, the sensor stays quiet and does nothing, allowing normal operation. The statement has been in Python for a long time (since the early versions in the 1990s). It became widely used around Python 1.5 (late 1990s) when it was documented as a way to include debugging checks. The concept of disabling assertions in optimized mode was also there early on – if you run Python with optimization (), it will strip out assert statements to potentially improve performance (so one should not rely on asserts for logic that must always execute). Over time, asserts have remained essentially the same, though there have been small changes: for example, Python 3 improved the error message when an assertion with a message fails (it now displays the message nicely). One thing to note: is a statement, not a function, and thus requires the condition after it (and the comma-separated message if any) rather than parentheses (writing is a valid expression but does nothing meaningful for the assert mechanism). The usage of in testing frameworks (like pytest) has also become popular — pytest rewrites assert statements to provide detailed introspection on failure. In summary, has been a stable feature used for debugging and testing, with the important understanding that it may be removed when running optimized bytecode.
Example 1: Using assert for a valid condition In this example, we use an assert to check a condition that is true. The assert passes silently and the program continues normally, printing the success message. x is small, continuing...
Example 2: Assert with a failing condition and message Here we use an assert that is supposed to fail. We check a condition that is false ( when is 10). We also provide a message explaining the failure. When the assertion fails, Python raises an and prints the message. The code after the assert is never reached in this case. Traceback (most recent call last): File "script.py", line 2, in <module> assert y < 5, "y is too large" AssertionError: y is too large In the output, we see a traceback indicating that an occurred, along with the message . This means the assert caught a situation that shouldn’t happen (in our test, we intentionally made it happen). In a real program, an assertion failure typically signals a bug that needs to be fixed. (If Python were run with , this assert would be skipped entirely.) The does nothing. It’s a placeholder statement that can be used whenever a statement is syntactically required but no action is needed. Writing results in no operation (NOP). It’s often used to create minimal class or function definitions, or as a stub in code blocks that are yet to be implemented. Essentially, tells Python: “do nothing here.” Think of as an , like a blank sign or an empty instruction. If you’re writing a to-do list and you have no item for a particular day, you might just leave it blank or write “nothing to do.” The statement is literally telling the interpreter “nothing to do here, move along.” The statement has been a part of Python since the very earliest versions. It was included to support the block-indentation syntax of Python – sometimes you need to have an indented block (after an , , , , etc.) but you may not want any code there (perhaps as a stub to fill in later). Without , leaving a block empty would be a syntax error. Over time, has had no changes in behavior; it’s as simple as it gets. It remains important for writing placeholder code. For instance, if you create a new function or class that you plan to implement later, you might write its header and put a inside to make it syntactically complete. Another use is in exception handling blocks where you deliberately want to ignore certain exceptions – writing will catch and ignore that error, effectively doing nothing in that case. The use of is straightforward and it hasn’t changed, reflecting Python’s philosophy of explicit syntax for a no-operation.
Example 1: Placeholder in function and class definitions Here we create an empty function and an empty class using to satisfy the syntax. We then show that calling the function does nothing (returns implicitly) and that we can instantiate the class (though it has no attributes or methods). None Created object of MyEmptyClass: <__main__.MyEmptyClass object at 0x7f...> In the output, the function returns (because it executed no return statement, just pass). The class can be instantiated, though it doesn’t actually do anything. The printed object is just a default object representation. This demonstrates how allows us to define placeholders that do nothing yet keep the code syntactically correct.
Example 2: Using pass in conditional or loop In this example, we use in an statement and in a loop. We want to skip certain cases without executing any code, so we put there. Checked x, moving on... Loop index 2, executing action. Here, since was 5, the block executes (doing nothing), and nothing is printed in the else. The program then prints the message after the if. In the loop, for and , the condition is true and is executed (no output, no action). When , the branch runs and prints the message. Using in this way lets us handle “do nothing” cases explicitly. The removes a binding from a name or deletes an item from a data structure. When you write , it , effectively removing that name from the current namespace (after this, using would cause a NameError unless it’s defined elsewhere). You can also use with indexing/slicing: for example, removes the element at index 2 from the list, and removes the entry with that key from a dictionary. If used on a slice like , it will remove that slice of elements from the list. In summary, is a statement that unbinds names or deletes elements from containers. Using is like throwing something away or removing a label. If a variable is a labeled reference to an object, is like tearing off that label – you can no longer refer to the object by that name (and if no other labels/references exist, the object will eventually be garbage-collected). When deleting from a list or dictionary, it’s like removing an item from a shelf: it’s no longer there, and the collection closes the gap around it. The statement has been in Python since the very early days (it’s part of Python’s core syntax in the 1990s). Its behavior of unbinding names is tied to Python’s memory model: when an object has no more references (e.g., after deleting a variable that referenced it), it becomes eligible for garbage collection. Over time, was extended to handle deleting slices (e.g., was allowed as Python evolved). One historical footnote: In Python 2.x, could also be used to delete an item from a list while iterating without needing an iterator method – but one has to be careful because removing items changes indices. Another detail is that you cannot delete some things: for instance, deleting a built-in name or an attribute that doesn’t exist raises an error. In Python 3, the meaning is the same: is straightforward. A recent nuance (though not a change in itself) is that Python’s garbage collector might not immediately free memory for certain objects even if you delete them, but logically the reference is gone. The statement’s syntax and purpose have remained the same throughout Python’s history.
Example 1: Deleting a variable (name unbinding) This example shows deleting a variable and then trying to use it. After using , the name is no longer defined, which results in a NameError if accessed. We catch the error to display it. NameError: name 'a' is not defined We see that after deleting , attempting to print causes a because the name has been removed from the namespace. This confirms that unbound the name . (If there were no except block catching the error, the program would have crashed with the NameError traceback.)
Example 2: Deleting items from a list and dictionary In this example, we use to remove an element from a list by index, and to remove a key-value pair from a dictionary by key. ['apple', 'cherry', 'date'] {'name': 'Alice', 'city': 'NYC'} After deleting, the list no longer contains "banana" (the list collapsed that gap, now index 1 is "cherry"). The dictionary no longer has the key. This shows how can remove elements from collections. If we tried to again after it’s gone, Python would raise a because the key is not found. Similarly, attempting to delete an index out of range in the list would raise an . So should be used on valid targets. The is used inside a function to exit the function and optionally pass a value backto the caller. When is executed, the function terminates at that point, and the (if provided) is evaluated and given as the function’s result. If a function executes without an expression (just ), or if it “falls off” the end (reaches the end of the function without encountering a return), it returns by default. The return statement is only valid inside function definitions (including methods); using it outside a function causes a syntax error. It provides a way to output a result from a function and to stop executing further code in that function. A in a function is like exiting a room with a gift. Imagine a function is a room where some processing happens. The return statement is the door out, and you can hand something (a result) to whoever opened the door (the caller). If you leave without handing anything (just return or end), the default “gift” given is (which is like saying “nothing to give”). The statement has always been a part of Python’s function mechanism (since Python 1.0 in the early 1990s). Its behavior is standard and has not changed: you can return any object, and returning exits the function. A note from history: In Python, unlike some languages, you cannot return multiple values by separating them with commas without automatically packing them into a tuple (e.g., actually returns a tuple ). That’s been a feature, not a bug, since early on – Python treats the comma as tuple construction in that context. Another detail: before generators were introduced, could not be used to yield values in a loop; that changed slightly with generators (where you use inside, and a bare to signal the end of generation). But the statement in a generator cannot include a value (in Python 2.x, it was illegal; Python 3.x allows in a generator, which raises StopIteration with that value internally – used rarely). In everyday use, remains straightforward. It’s how functions deliver results. Over the years, best practices encourage using to make functions produce a single, clear outcome and avoid multiple unrelated exit points if possible for readability.
Example 1: Returning a value from a function This function adds two numbers and returns the result. We call it and use the returned value. If we didn’t use , the function would return None by default, which is not what we want for an addition operation.The function uses to send back the sum of the two arguments. The caller receives 7 and we print it. If we had additional code after the return in the function, it would never execute.
Example 2: Early return and default return In this example, we demonstrate how can be used to exit a function early. We write a function to find the first even number in a list. It returns as soon as it finds one. If it finishes looping without finding any even number, it returns implicitly (no explicit return at the end).In the first call, the function encounters 6 and returns it, so we see printed. In the second call, the loop finds no even number; the function ends without hitting a return, so it returns by default. We print that result, which shows up as . This illustrates both using to exit early and how a missing return is equivalent to . The is used inside a special kind of function called a . It allows the function to produce a sequence of values over time, pausing after each yield and resuming where it left off when the generator is called again. When a function contains , it’s not a normal function anymore; it becomes a generator function. Calling such a function returns a generator object. Using in the function will emit a value to the caller (like a return, but the function state is saved), and suspend the function’s execution at that point. The next time the generator is asked for a value (e.g., via or a loop), the function continues execution right after the last yield. This continues until the function returns or reaches the end, at which point the generator is exhausted (raises StopIteration). Generators are a powerful feature for creating iterators with state that don’t require building the entire list in memory at once. Using is like a in a TV series. Imagine a generator function is a TV show, and each is an episode that you release to the audience. After yielding an episode, the show goes on a break (the function pauses). When the audience asks for the next episode (calls the generator again), the show resumes from where it left off and produces the next episode. The show's state (characters, plot) is remembered between episodes. Finally, when the show ends (the function returns or completes), there are no more episodes (values) to yield. The statement was introduced to Python in as an optional feature (via from __future__ import generators) and became fully available in Python 2.3 (What’s New in Python 2.2 — Python 3.13.2 documentation). This addition (PEP 255) brought generator functions to Python, allowing easier creation of iterators. The early implementation allowed basic generators that could yield values. Later, in Python 2.5 (2006, PEP 342), generators were enhanced: they could not only yield values out, but also receive values sent in via and even propagate exceptions. Another big enhancement came with , which introduced the syntax (PEP 380) to delegate to sub-generators, making complex generator pipelines easier. Throughout these changes, the core behavior of remained: it suspends function execution, preserving local state, and can be resumed. A generator function can yield multiple times, whereas a normal function can only return once. The introduction of was a milestone that influenced language features in other languages and was later built upon by Python’s async features (which use similar to yield). In modern Python, is commonly used for streaming data, reading large files incrementally, or producing infinite sequences. It has not changed in syntax since introduction, and remains a key part of Python’s iterator protocol.
Example 1: Simple generator using yield This generator function yields a series of values (from 1 up to a given number). We use a loop to iterate through the generator’s output. The advantage is that the generator yields one value at a time and maintains state between yields without constructing the full list in memory.When is called, it returns a generator object. The loop then repeatedly asks this generator for the next value. The generator function runs and yields 1, then pauses. The loop prints 1. Next iteration, the generator resumes right after the yield, yields 2, pauses; the loop prints 2. Next, yields 3, pauses. After yielding 3 and looping back, the in finishes, and the generator function exits, raising StopIteration internally which tells the loop to stop. This demonstrates the basic use of yield to create a sequence of values.
Example 2: Generator that remembers state between yields Here’s a generator that demonstrates the function state being preserved between yields. We print messages before and after yielding to show when the function is active. We also manually fetch values using to illustrate the pause/resume behavior. Start of generator 1 Between yields 2 End of generator No more values Let’s break down what happened:The first call started execution. The generator printed "Start of generator", then hit . It paused there, returning the value 1. That’s why we see "Start of generator" then on the output.The second resumed execution right after the first yield. The generator printed "Between yields", then hit . It paused again, returning 2. So we see "Between yields" then .The third resumes after the second yield. The generator prints "End of generator" and then reaches the end of the function (no more yields), thus raising . We catch this and print "No more values".This example clearly shows how the state is maintained: variables, the position in code, etc., all persist between yields. Each is like a checkpoint that we can resume from later. Generators allow writing code that produces a sequence of results over time, which can be more memory-efficient and convenient than building the whole result list at once. The is used to intentionally. Its syntax is raise [ExceptionType(value)] or simply (inside an exception handler, to re-raise the current exception). When you execute , Python immediately interrupts normal flow and creates an exception object. If this exception isn’t caught by a surrounding , it propagates upward (unwinding the call stack) and, if unhandled, will terminate the program with a traceback. Typically, you raise exceptions to signal error conditions or unexpected situations in your code (for example, raise ValueError("invalid input") if a function gets an argument it cannot handle). The raised exception can be any class derived from (usually or subclasses). In Python 3, the syntax is raise ExceptionType("detail message") or (to rethrow). In older Python 2, there was a different syntax (raise ExceptionType, message), but that’s no longer used in Python 3. Using is like in a building. When you raise an exception, you’re signaling that something is wrong or some special condition has occurred. The normal activities (normal code flow) stop immediately, and the “alarm” (exception) is sent up to be handled by some exception handler (like people responding to the alarm). If nobody handles it, the program “evacuates” (stops with an error message). The ability to raise exceptions has been in Python from the beginning, as part of its exception handling model (early 1990s). Over time, there were some changes in syntax and usage. In Python 2, you could raise exceptions using a different syntax (for example, raise IOError, "file not found" or even raising string exceptions in very old Python versions before 2.0). By Python 2.5, raising string exceptions was deprecated and removed in Python 3. In Python 3, the syntax was cleaned up to raise ExceptionType(value) only, and exceptions must inherit from (usually from ). Another addition in Python 3 is the ability to chain exceptions with raise Exception(...) from original_exception, which allows you to indicate an exception is a direct consequence of another. This helps with debugging by preserving the traceback of the original error. The raise statement otherwise works the same: it causes an immediate exception. The semantics of how exceptions propagate (searching for a matching except block) have remained consistent. In practice, developers use for error handling and for sanity checks. The evolution of removed old forms (like the comma-separated syntax) to make code cleaner. But conceptually, raising an exception has always meant signaling an error condition up the call stack.
Example 1: Raising a custom exception for invalid input In this example, we have a function that checks an age value. If the age is negative, it’s not valid, so we with an error message. We call the function in a try/except to catch the exception and handle it gracefully. Error: Age cannot be negative Here, triggers the raise ValueError("Age cannot be negative"). This immediately stops and jumps to the except block. Our except catches it (we used a broad catch for demonstration) and prints an error message containing the exception’s message. The program doesn’t crash because we handled the exception. If we didn’t catch it, this would have produced a traceback and terminated execution.
Example 2: Raising an exception to stop execution (uncaught) This example demonstrates an uncaught exception. We simply do a without catching it. This will terminate the program and show a traceback. Traceback (most recent call last): File "script.py", line 1, in <module> raise RuntimeError("Something went wrong") RuntimeError: Something went wrong As shown, when the raise RuntimeError("Something went wrong") executes, it produces a traceback. The second print statement is never reached. The Python interpreter prints the traceback with the file name, line number, and the exception type and message. In a real program, you would typically have this inside a try/except if you wanted to recover or handle it; otherwise, as in this snippet, it will crash the program.(If you wanted to see exception chaining, you could do something like raise RuntimeError("New error") from original_error, but that is an advanced usage. By default, just or raise ExceptionType(message) is the standard way to throw an error.) The is used inside loops ( or ) to terminate the loop immediately. When is executed, the program jumps out of the innermost loop in which it appears, skipping any remaining iterations. The loop is done and control resumes at the first statement after the loop. It does affect outer loops (if you have nested loops, it only breaks the one it’s directly in). is typically used to exit a loop early when a certain condition is met (for example, finding an item you were searching for, so you can stop searching). Hitting in a loop is like or exiting a room before the meeting is over. Imagine you’re searching for a word in a book and you decide to stop as soon as you find the first occurrence. Once found, you of the searching process. Similarly, says "I’m done with this loop now, stop immediately." The statement has been part of Python from the very beginning and works similarly to break in many other languages. It’s a straightforward control flow mechanism. Historically, one quirk in Python 2 was that you couldn’t use break inside a block (in a construct) due to an implementation limitation. This restriction was lifted in Python 3.8 (What's New In Python 3.8 — Python 3.13.2 documentation, so now you can use break even in a finally clause of a loop’s try. Another relevant feature is that Python has loop-clauses: a or can have an clause that executes only if the loop wasn’t terminated by a break. This has existed for a long time (since Python 2.x), and it’s often used in search loops to indicate "not found" (the else executes if no break happened). Aside from that, has consistently performed the same action of ending loops early. It’s important to note that using break outside of a loop is a syntax error. Over time, best practices around break (and continue) are mostly about readability – for example, avoiding deep nested loops with multiple breaks if possible. But the fundamental behavior of break is unchanged in Python’s history.
Example 1: Using break in a loop to stop early In this example, we loop from 1 to 5 but we decide to break out of the loop when we reach 3. Only the numbers 1 and 2 will be fully processed, and the loop will terminate once 3 is encountered (so 3 is not printed, and 4 and 5 are never reached). 1 2 Found 3, breaking out Loop ended As we see, the loop printed 1 and 2. When became 3, the condition triggered, it printed the message and executed . The loop then stopped completely. The final print statement after the loop confirms that we have exited the loop. (Notice that 3 was not printed inside the loop body because we broke before reaching the for that iteration, and 4,5 were never processed at all.)
Example 2: Loop with else and break Here we demonstrate the use of a loop in conjunction with break. We search a list for a specific value. If we find it, we break out. If we finish the loop without finding it, the clause will execute.In this case, the list does not contain 10. The loop goes through all values (5,7,11,15), none trigger the break. After the loop completes normally, the clause executes, printing the "not found" message. If the were in the list, say 11, the output would be just and the else clause would be skipped (because the loop was broken out of). This example shows a Python idiom: using to handle the "searched but not found" scenario, relying on break. The is used inside loops to skip the rest of the current iteration and jump to the next iteration. When is executed, Python immediately stops the current cycle of the loop and goes back to the loop’s start to handle the next iteration (if any). In a loop, it moves to the next item; in a loop, it jumps to re-checking the condition. Any code below the in that iteration is skipped. It’s typically used when you want to skip certain items based on a condition but still continue looping through the rest. is like saying "skip this one and move on". Imagine you’re reading pages of a book and you decide to skip any page that has a coffee stain. When you see a stained page, you immediately skip to the next page, ignoring the rest of the stained page. Similarly, in a loop, says “ignore the remainder of this iteration and go to the next one.” The statement has been present in Python from the beginning, functioning as a companion to . As mentioned in the break section, historically Python didn’t allow inside a clause (in a try/finally) until Python 3.8 due to an implementation quirk; Python 3.8 removed that restriction (8. Compound statements — Python 3.13.2 documentation). That was a minor technical detail—most users didn’t encounter it often. The usage of has remained the same. It’s worth noting that overly liberal use of (and break) can sometimes make loops harder to follow, so it’s often used in simple scenarios or with clear conditions. Python’s loop-else construct is affected by continue only in that continue does not terminate the loop, so the loop’s else will still run if no break happens. Other than that, simply short-circuits the loop iteration. Its behavior has been stable across Python versions. Just like break, using continue outside of a loop is a syntax error. In summary, is a straightforward control statement that’s changed little over Python’s history, aside from now being allowed in more scenarios with Python 3.8+.
Example 1: Skipping even numbers in a loop In this loop, we only want to process odd numbers. We use to skip over any even number immediately, so only odds are printed.The loop goes 1 through 5. When is even (2 and 4), the triggers and executes, which jumps to the next iteration (so it doesn’t reach the print for those values). For odd (1,3,5), the continue is not hit and the number gets printed. This way, we filtered out the even numbers using continue.
Example 2: Using continue in a while loop Here we use a while loop and to skip a particular iteration. We increment a counter and want to skip printing the number 3 for some reason.In the output, you see 1, 2, and then it jumps to 4, 5. The value 3 is not printed because when was 3, the caused the loop to skip the print and go straight to the next iteration (where i becomes 4). Notice that we incremented the continue check. If we had the after the continue, we would have had an infinite loop because continue would skip the increment. This example underscores that any code after in that iteration won’t run, so loop variables should be managed accordingly. The loop eventually prints 4 and 5 normally and then ends when is false. The is used to bring modules or specific attributes of modules into your current namespace. There are a couple of forms: – this imports the module as a whole, meaning you'll use to access things in it.import module_name as alias – same as above but assigns the module to a local name (alias) for convenience.There is also a variant handled in the next section (). The statement finds the module (a file or a built-in module) in the Python module search path, initializes it (executes its top-level code if not already loaded), and creates a module object bound to the name in your namespace. If the module was already imported previously, by default import will just bind the existing module (to avoid re-running code unnecessarily). Modules typically contain functions, classes, or variables that you can then use in your code. Importing a module is like . Imagine each Python module is a toolbox full of tools (functions, classes). When you do , you’re bringing in the math toolbox so you can use its tools, but you still open it by name (prefixing with ). If you use , you’ve just given the toolbox a nickname for convenience. You can think of a module as a reference book and as going to the library and picking up that book to have on hand. Module importing has been in Python since the very start (modules are one of Python’s core features). Over time, some improvements were made (for instance, packages and the package import system were introduced in Python 1.5, and relative imports were tweaked in Python 2 and 3). The basic syntax hasn’t changed. In early Python, you always had to import full module names; with the introduction of packages (directories with ), the import system got the ability to handle nested modules (like ). A significant change came with Python 3 relative imports: in Python 2, could sometimes resolve to a module in the same package (implicit relative import). Python 3 made all absolute by default, unless you use a dot for explicit relative import. Another historical addition is the library which allows dynamic import of modules and reloading, but that’s an internal detail. The syntax was added to allow aliasing (this existed in Python 2 as well). Also, Python’s import mechanism caches modules in to avoid redundant loads. The statement has always thrown an (ModuleNotFoundError in Python 3.6+) if a module can’t be found. Performance-wise, the import system has evolved to improve module search and loading (like .pyc files for caching bytecode). But from a user perspective, writing remains the straightforward way to use modules across all Python versions.

We then use to get today’s date. The output shows the date (in ISO format). The exact date will vary depending on when you run the code (here it was 2025-03-03). Using aliases like can make code cleaner or avoid name conflicts. Note that without , if we tried to use , it would error because isn’t defined by default; it lives in the datetime module.

If found, it executed that file, created a module object, and bound it to the name . We then accessed its function. If the module wasn’t found, Python would raise an .
