14. The Statement
The allows you to import specific names from a module directly into your current namespace. The syntax is from module_name import name1, name2, .... After executing this, you can use and directly without the module prefix. There’s also an alias form: from module import name as alias. You can import multiple names separated by commas, or use (asterisk) to import everything (though using is discouraged in real code because it can clutter the namespace and make it unclear which names come from the module). Essentially, means bring the object Y from module X into the local namespace, so it’s as if you defined Y in this file. This is useful for convenience or when you only need a few things from a module. If brings in the math toolbox, then is like taking just the “sqrt” tool out of the toolbox and putting it on your workbench. Now you can use directly without referencing the whole math toolbox each time. It’s selecting specific items for direct access. The syntax has been in Python for a very long time (it was present in Python 1.x). It has remained mostly the same. A subtle historical point: in Python 2, using inside a function was not allowed unless it was at the top of the module, but this is a niche detail. More importantly, Python 2 allowed implicit relative imports (e.g., behavior without dot), but Python 3 requires explicit relative imports using dot notation. For absolute imports, works the same in Python 2 and 3 (except Python 3 will not accidentally find a local module named “module” shadowing a standard one, because it does absolute by default). Performance-wise, importing specific names or using might have minor differences, but generally, Python’s import mechanism handles them similarly by first loading the module object (if not loaded) and then performing attribute access. One enhancement in Python 3: if you do , and is not actually in the module, you get an right away (this was the case in Python 2 as well). The star import () evolved such that it respects the module’s variable to decide what to import. However, best practices today avoid star imports in most cases. The syntax also handles importing submodules (e.g., from package import submodule). Historically, if you wanted to import a module as a whole with an alias, you use the regular import alias syntax (not from-import). The from-import is specifically for pulling out attributes or submodules.
Example 1: Import specific functions from a module We import just the function from , and two attributes from in one line ( and ). Then we use them without the prefix. 120 3.141592653589793 5.0 Here, returns 120. We imported and as well, so prints the value of π and gives 5.0. We did not need to write or because using the from-import brought those names into the local namespace. If we had not imported and tried to use it, we’d get a NameError because only was imported in the first line. Each from-import only brings the specified names.
Example 2: Using alias in from-import and wildcard import In this example, we show how to alias an imported name, and (for demonstration) how a wildcard import might work.First, we did from math import pow as power. This means we can use in place of . The output is 2 to the power of 3. Next, brings in all public names from math. After that, we can call and use without qualification. gives 1.0. Normally, is discouraged because it dumps a lot of names into your namespace and you may not know which module they came from or if they override existing names. In fact, many linters will warn against it. However, it’s sometimes used in interactive settings or certain specialized scenarios. In regular code, it’s better to import only what you need or import the module and use the prefix for clarity. The aliasing feature () is helpful when the original name is too long or conflicts with another name in your code.
15. (from __future__ import ...)
A is a special import that enables new language features which are not compatible with the current interpreter’s default behavior. The syntax is from __future__ import feature_name. These statements are recognized by the compiler and act at compile-time to modify how the code is parsed or executed. Future statements allow you to use features that will become standard in a future Python version but can be opted into in earlier versions. Each feature in has a specific Python version where it becomes the default. Common examples from the past include from __future__ import division (which made the operator do true division as it does in Python 3) and from __future__ import print_function (to use the Python 3 style in Python 2). In modern Python (3.x), some future features still exist for backward compatibility or gradual introduction (like future in Python 3.7+, or ). A future statement must appear at the top of the module (before any other code except docstrings or comments). A future import is like a preview mode or compatibility switch. Imagine a car manufacturer offering a new type of fuel but your car by default uses the old fuel. A future statement is like a retrofit kit that lets your car run on the new fuel now, before all cars switch to it in the next model. It’s opting into a future standard early.Absolute import behavior (PEP 328) in Python 2.5.The print function and unicode literals in Python 2.6/2.7 leading up to Python 3.Annotations (PEP 563) in Python 3.7 (postponed evaluation of annotations).Each import has two version numbers associated: the version when it’s initially optional and the version when it becomes mandatory (no import needed). For example, was optional in 2.2 and mandatory in 3.0 ( — Future statement definitions — Python 3.13.2 documentation). In Python 3.x, most of the past future features are already standard. The module still exists to allow backward compatibility and new experimentations. For instance, Python 3.10 introduced no new future flags, but Python 3.7’s from __future__ import annotations is still available in 3.10+ (in 3.11, they decided not to make it mandatory yet). Using future imports in modern code is mainly for things like or if writing code that should run on multiple Python versions with different default behaviors. Historically, future statements played a crucial role in the transition from Python 2 to 3, allowing code to opt-in to Python 3 behaviors while still running on Python 2.
Example 1: Using a future import for true division (Python 2.x scenario) In Python 3, true division is default, but let's demonstrate what it would have been like in Python 2. In Python 2, would give 2 (floor division for integers) unless you import division from . The example below shows intended behavior under Python 2.7:Output (if run in Python 2.x):Without the future import, Python 2 would have printed (because it would perform integer division). The future statement from __future__ import division tells the Python 2 interpreter to use Python 3’s division behavior, so yields 2.5. In Python 3, this import is unnecessary since true division is already the default. This future import was available starting in Python 2.2 ( — Future statement definitions — Python 3.13.2 documentation) and became standard in Python 3.0.
Example 2: Using a future import for annotations (Python 3.7+ feature) Python 3.7 introduced an optional future feature (PEP 563), which changes how function/variable annotations are stored (as strings rather than evaluated objects). This can improve performance by not evaluating annotation expressions at function definition time. Here's an example demonstrating its effect: {'name': 'str', 'return': 'str'} Normally, without the future import (in Python 3.10 for instance), would be {'name': <class 'str'>, 'return': <class 'str'>} where the values are actual types. But with from __future__ import annotations, the annotations are kept as un-evaluated strings ( instead of ). This was a future feature to postpone evaluation of annotations to speed up runtime and avoid issues with forward references. As of Python 3.11, this future import is still honored (the switch to make it default was deferred to a later version or a different approach). The example shows that by using the future import, our annotations are stored as strings. This doesn’t change program logic but could affect tools or introspection. It's a modern example of a future statement in action.(These future imports must be at the top of the file. If you try to put them later, they won’t work and Python will complain. They essentially communicate with the compiler.) The declares that certain variable names are to be treated as global variables (module-level) inside a function. The syntax is used at the top of a function’s body (before using the variables). Normally in Python, when you assign to a variable inside a function, it’s considered a new local variable by default. If you want to assign to a variable that’s defined outside the function (in the global scope), you must declare it as global in that function. The statement does not define or create a global variable by itself; it just tells Python that any assignments to those names refer to the global scope’s variables. It can list multiple names separated by commas. Using global allows you to modify module-level variables from within a function. Declaring a variable as global inside a function is like telling a function to use a billboard instead of a sticky note. Normally, a function writes on its own sticky notes (local variables) when you assign values. By marking a name as global, you’re telling the function, “No, that name refers to the big billboard outside (the global variable), go write there.” So changes reflect on the billboard that everyone can see, not just inside the function’s private notebook. The concept of global variables has been in Python from the start, and so has the statement. In early Python, the scoping rules were simpler (only local and global scopes existed until nested functions got closure support around Python 2.2). The global statement allowed inner functions (or any function) to modify a variable in the module’s global scope. This has remained unchanged. One limitation historically was that could only refer to module-level (truly global) variables, not to variables in an enclosing function scope – that's why Python 3 introduced for that purpose. In Python 2, if you wanted to assign to a variable in an outer (but not global) scope, there was no direct way (people used mutable containers or other tricks). Python 3.0 added to address that gap. The keyword itself has not changed; it’s a simple part of the syntax. It’s worth noting that if you just read a global variable in a function (and don’t assign to it), you don’t need the global statement. But if you plan to assign to it, you must declare it global beforehand, otherwise Python will treat it as a new local and not refer to the global one. Over time, some style guides discourage heavy use of global variables because they can make code harder to manage, but the mechanism is there when needed.
Example 1: Modifying a global variable inside a function We have a global counter. We create a function that increments it using the global statement. Without declaring , the assignment inside would make a local variable named count and not affect the global one.The output is 2 because each call to increased the module-level by 1. If we omitted inside the function, Python would have thrown an UnboundLocalError at because it would think we’re trying to use a local variable before assigning any value to it (since was assigned in the function, Python would treat it as local by default, making the invalid as it had no initial local value). By declaring it global, we ensure refers to the variable defined outside. After two increments, the global changed from 0 to 2.
Example 2: Using multiple globals and interacting with locals In this example, we show that global only affects the names listed. We use a global and a local variable with the same name to illustrate the difference (this is generally not a good practice, but for demonstration).We declared in the function, so when we assign to x, it affects the global . Thus, after calling , the global becomes 100.We did not declare . So when we assign inside the function, it treats as a local variable (this does not touch the global ). That local exists only inside and goes away after the function. The global remains unchanged at 20. The output confirms this: was changed globally, stayed as it was. This example illustrates that you must list each name you want to be global. Also, it shows how a global name can be shadowed by a local if not declared global. The is used in nested functions to indicate that a variable refers to a previously bound variable in the nearest enclosing function scope (not the global scope). In other words, it allows inner functions to assign to variables that are in the outer function’s scope. The syntax is inside a function that is nested within another function. By declaring a name nonlocal, assignments to that name will affect the variable in the outer function scope (the closest scope where it’s defined) instead of creating a new local variable. This is similar to global, but for enclosing (non-global) scopes. It only works for nested function scopes, not for the module scope (for module scope you’d use global). If we use the previous analogy of sticky notes and billboards: think of as writing on your parent function’s whiteboard. The inner function normally has its own notepad (locals). Marking a variable as nonlocal means “don’t use the notepad, use the whiteboard in the next room (outer function)”. It’s like a child asking to use a parent’s variable rather than making their own copy. The keyword was introduced in to fill a gap in Python’s variable scoping (Referencing local variable before assignment results in unexpected ...). Before Python 3, if you had a nested function and wanted to rebind a variable from the outer function, you had no direct way. People resorted to using mutable objects (like lists or dictionaries) or making that variable global. PEP 3104 added to solve this. Since its introduction, nonlocal has remained the same in functionality. It’s only valid in a nested function context. Attempting to use nonlocal for a variable that isn’t actually in an enclosing function (or is global) will cause an error. The adoption of nonlocal helped write closures that modify state enclosed in outer scopes, which is useful for certain patterns (like making function factories or to preserve state between calls without using globals). Nonlocal variables must already exist in the outer function scope when declared (you can’t use nonlocal to create a new variable in an outer scope; it must be defined in the outer function). This keyword is relatively straightforward and hasn’t changed since Python 3.0. It remains a key piece for closure use-cases.
Example 1: Using nonlocal to modify an outer variable Here we have an outer function that defines a variable, and an inner function that uses to modify that variable. We call the inner function to see the effect on the outer variable. inner x: 8 outer x: 8 Explanation: Initially, in is 5. The function declares , meaning it will refer to the in 's scope. The line thus increments the outer . When we call , it prints (since 5+3=8). After that, back in , we print , confirming that the outer function’s was indeed updated by the inner function. Without , the statement inside would have tried to create a local and given an UnboundLocalError (because it would treat x as local but it's used in an expression before assignment). The statement enables this closure behavior.
Example 2: Nonlocal vs global This example shows a scenario with a global, an outer function variable, and an inner function. It highlights how nonlocal and global target different scopes. outer_func y: 10 global y: 1 In , we assign . This creates a local in , unrelated to the global (the global is shadowed within outer_func).In , we declare . That means when we do in inner_func, it will assign to the global .We call . It sets the global from 100 to 1.We then print inside outer_func. Outer_func’s own is still 10, unaffected by inner_func (inner_func was dealing with global y).After outer_func returns, we print the global , which is now 1. This shows the difference: in inner_func did not touch outer_func’s local . If we wanted to affect the outer_func’s y from inner_func, we would use (and not have a global y at all in this example). This demonstrates that global and nonlocal apply to different scopes. In summary, nonlocal is for the nearest enclosing function scope, and global is for the module scope.(If we changed to use instead, it would have modified the outer_func’s y from 10 to 1, and left the global y at 100. But nonlocal wouldn’t be allowed here unless we remove the global y or use a different name, because currently the nearest enclosing scope’s y is 10 (outer_func’s y), which is fine, but we also have a global y which might confuse the scenario. Generally, avoid using the same name for globals and enclosing variables to prevent confusion.)
18. The Statement (Python 2) / Function (Python 3)
In Python 2, was a that printed to standard output. In Python 3, became a . While semantically similar (both output text), the syntax differs. The old print statement in Python 2 looked like: or (without parentheses and with special rules for ending comma or >> for redirection). The new print function is used like . The purpose in both cases is to display text or representations of objects to the console (or another file/stream if specified). Because Python 3's print is a function, it has flexible arguments: print(*objects, sep=' ', end='\n', file=sys.stdout, flush=False). The transition was made to make more consistent and powerful (and to remove it as a keyword). The print statement/function is like a for your program. It's how your program communicates out to the world (the console, in most cases). In Python 2, it was like a built-in command with its own syntax. In Python 3, it’s like using a megaphone that you can configure (the print function, which is a tool you call with parentheses and can adjust its settings like separator, end character, etc.). Originally, in Python 1 and 2, was one of the few statements (like , , etc.) that weren't just assignments or flow control but actually performed output. It was very convenient but had some quirks (for example, with a trailing comma in Python 2 would suppress the newline, and syntax was used to direct output to a file). With Python 3.0, as part of a larger effort to make the language more uniform, was changed to a normal function (PEP 3105) (PEP 3105 – Make print a function | peps.python.org). This meant removing it from the syntax as a special-case keyword. The change allowed features like specifying the end or separator string directly as function parameters instead of using special syntax. It was a significant backward-incompatible change. For a period, Python 2.6+ allowed from __future__ import print_function so that you could use the Python 3 style in Python 2 (What’s New In Python 3.0 — Python 3.13.2 documentation). As of Python 3, is entirely a function, and any usage of it as a statement (without parentheses) is a syntax error. The historical shift also means that in Python 3, you can override or rebind the name (because it's just a variable in builtins) if you wanted, whereas in Python 2 it was a keyword and couldn't be easily overridden. The function in Python 3 gained additional flexibility (like to forcibly flush output). Over time (Python 3.7+), remains essentially the same function. The key point is that code from Python 2 that uses statements needs to be converted to calls for Python 3. This is one of the most visible syntax changes between the versions.
Example 1: Python 2 style print vs Python 3 print() The following shows how one would print in Python 2 versus Python 3. (This is for illustration; the Python 2 style will not run in Python 3 without the future import.)In Python 2, you could write and it would print (with a space by default between arguments, and a newline at the end). In Python 3, the equivalent is . The output shown is for the Python 3 code. If you tried the Python 2 line in Python 3, you’d get a syntax error. In Python 2, the print statement automatically adds a space between items and a newline at the end, similar to the print function’s default behavior. The difference is mostly syntactical here.
Example 2: Using print() function features (Python 3) Python 3’s print being a function offers more control. Here we demonstrate using the (separator) and parameters, which were not available with the old print statement (at least not in as clear a way). Monday | Tuesday | Wednesday Hello World! In the first print call, we printed three words separated by . Normally, print separates multiple arguments with a space by default, but by specifying , we changed it. The result is those words joined by . In the second and third print calls, we demonstrate changing the argument. The first prints "Hello" but ends with a space instead of a newline. Therefore, the next print’s output will continue on the same line. The next print("World", end="!\n") prints "World" and ends with an exclamation and a newline (we explicitly put ). The combined output of these two prints is "Hello World!" on one line (the first print output "Hello " then second output "World!"). In Python 2, to achieve the same effect, you might have written (with a comma to avoid newline) and then etc., which was less flexible. The Python 3 approach is more powerful and clear.
Example 3: Legacy Python 2 print to file vs Python 3 In Python 2, you could direct print output to a file using the syntax print >>file_obj, "message". In Python 3, you do this by print("message", file=file_obj). For example:(to standard error, not standard output)This will print the message to the standard error stream (which in many environments appears separately from standard output). In Python 2, you’d write print >> sys.stderr, "Error occurred!". The new syntax is more consistent with how you pass other parameters to functions. This example shows the modern way, leveraging the parameter of the print function.
19. The Statement (Python 2) / Function (Python 3) was a that executed a string of Python code dynamically. In Python 3, is a function (actually a built-in) that does the same thing. The functionality is to take Python code, typically as a string (or compiled code object), and execute it as if it were part of the program. The syntax in Python 2 was or exec code_str in globals_dict, locals_dict for specifying context. In Python 3, you call exec(code_str, globals_dict, locals_dict) as a function (it’s actually a built-in that acts like a function). allows dynamic execution of Python code, which means you can construct code at runtime and run it. It has access to the current scopes via optional dictionaries for global and local namespace. It returns (it doesn’t produce a value, it just executes code for its side effects). is like a embedded in your program. It’s as if your program can write a script on the fly and then run that script immediately. This is powerful but also potentially dangerous if used with untrusted input (like letting someone type code that you exec can run arbitrary code). So, it’s akin to having a magic book where any text you write becomes real actions in your program. In Python 2, was a keyword/statement. You would use it without parentheses. For example: would execute that code, assigning 5 to x. In Python 3, as part of the language changes (PEP 3105 made print a function, and similarly PEP 3110 and others cleaned up exception syntax and exec), was removed as a statement and turned into a function-like built-in (What’s New In Python 3.0 — Python 3.13.2 documentation. Now you do . The functionality is largely the same, but as a function it takes arguments for the global and local dictionaries to execute in. The removal of as a statement also closed some subtle parsing issues and made the grammar simpler. Another note: Python 2 had an function for directly executing a file’s content; in Python 3, you achieve that via . Over the years, direct use of exec has been discouraged except for specific dynamic needs, because it can make code harder to understand and maintain, and it poses security risks if not handled carefully. But it remains a powerful tool. For example, some code generators or educational tools might use exec to run generated code. Historically, the statement in Python 2 couldn’t be easily sandboxed (and similarly for exec in Python 3, true sandboxing is hard in pure Python). The conversion to a function didn’t drastically change how exec works under the hood; it primarily changed syntax. One difference is that in Python 2, could be used in a local scope and it would by default modify that local scope. In Python 3, because exec is a function, modifying local variables of the caller via exec is tricky (exec on a string in the current local scope doesn’t automatically write to the local variables of the caller due to how Python’s scoping works; you typically provide a context dict). This is a subtle difference that one might encounter when porting code. But for the most part, in Python 3 has the same effect as in Python 2 in a global context or when using an explicit dictionary. Summarily, the big historical change was turning it from a statement to a function in Python 3, as part of making the language more regular.
Example 1: Executing dynamically created code We have a string containing Python code. We use to run it. After exec, any definitions made by that code (variables, functions) will be available in our scope (if we exec in the global namespace).Explanation: The string contains three lines of Python code. The call executes those lines as if they were written in the program at that point. It creates variables and , adds them, and prints the result. We see 15 printed. After this, we could also access and in our Python environment (they would exist in the global namespace following exec). This demonstrates how exec can define variables on the fly. Note that using exec like this can be risky if comes from an untrusted source, because it can execute arbitrary commands. But it's useful for dynamic programming tasks.
Example 2: Using exec to define a function dynamically This example shows exec creating a new function at runtime and then calling it. We compose a function definition as a string, execute it, and then use the newly created function.Here, the string in defines a function . After , the function exists in the current global namespace. We then call and it prints "Hello, Bob". This shows how exec can be used to generate functions or classes dynamically. While you could achieve similar results by other means (like using closures or factory functions), exec provides a very direct way to materialize code from text. Again, for security and clarity, this is typically done in controlled situations.
Example 3: Using exec with a custom namespace In this example, we’ll execute code in a separate dictionary to avoid polluting our global namespace.x in global? False x in namespace? 42 We created an empty dictionary . By passing to , the code executes such that is set in that dictionary rather than in our global variables. After exec, we check that is not in globals (so our module’s global scope was untouched), but in the namespace dict, is 42. This way, exec can be used in a sandboxed manner (to some extent) or at least to control where the executed code has effect. If the code had a print statement, it would still print to standard output, but variables and definitions went into that provided dictionary. Using exec(code, globals_dict, locals_dict) allows fine control of execution context.This example underscores that in Python 3 is a function that can take separate global and local dictionaries. In Python 2, one would write exec code in globals_dict, locals_dict with slightly different syntax. The outcome is the same idea.Got it! Here's the section in that style:
Next Steps with Python Simple Statements You’ve just gone through a full guide on . Now’s the time to make it stick. Keep this close—print it out, bookmark it, or read through it during lunch. The goal is to make these statements feel like second nature.Set aside a paper copy. There’s something about seeing it on the page. Mark it up. Highlight the parts that trip you up. Keep it at your desk or in your bag.Take five or ten minutes when you can. A lunch break or a quiet moment is perfect. Skim the examples. See if you can spot how they work without looking at the notes.Bookmark for Quick ChecksSave this guide in your browser. When you’re coding and hit a wall—check back. Forgot how works? Need a reminder on ? It’s all here.Pick one statement a day. Write it out. Change it. Break it. Fix it. You don’t need a big project—just a few lines to get the feel for it.Watch for Them in the WildAs you read other people’s code, notice which simple statements show up. How do they use them? What tricks do they have? Borrow what works.Stick with it. Small steps add up fast. Before long, these statements won’t just make sense—they’ll feel like old friends.
What’s Your Take on Python Simple Statements? Now that you've seen how work, I'd love to hear what you think. Are there any statements you reach for often? Are some still a bit unclear?What’s been the most useful or surprising in your own work? Have you found any clever ways to use them?Share your thoughts below. Your tips and questions help others learn, too. What do you like? is an American software engineer and writer based in Los Angeles. Mike writes about technology leadership and holds degrees in Linguistics and Industrial Automation. More about Mike Vincent

item.title: Python while Loops: Repeating Tasks Conditionally
item.author: Real Python
item.content:encoded: Python’s loop enables you to execute a block of code repeatedly as long as a given condition remains true. Unlike loops, which iterate a known number of times, loops are ideal for situations where the number of iterations isn’t known upfront.Loops are a pretty useful construct in Python, so learning how to write and use them is a great skill for you as a Python developer.By the end of this tutorial, you’ll understand that: used to initiate a loop that repeats a block of code as long as a condition is true.A loop works by evaluating a condition at the start of each iteration. If the condition is true, then the loop executes. Otherwise, it terminates. loops are useful when the number of iterations is unknown, such as waiting for a condition to change or continuously processing user input. in Python creates an infinite loop that continues until a statement or external interruption occurs.Python lacks a built-in do-while loop, but you can emulate it using a loop with a statement for conditional termination.With this knowledge, you’re prepared to write effective loops in your Python programs, handling a wide range of iteration needs. Test your knowledge with our interactive “Python while Loops: Repeating Tasks Conditionally” quiz. You’ll receive a score upon completion to help you track your learning progress:In this quiz, you'll test your understanding of Python's while loop. This loop allows you to execute a block of code repeatedly as long as a given condition remains true. Understanding how to use while loops effectively is a crucial skill for any Python developer.Getting Started With Python LoopsIn programming, loops are statements that allow you to repeat a given set of operations a number of times. In practice, you’ll find two main types of loops: loops are mostly used to iterate a number of times, which is common when you’re processing data collections with a specific number of data items. loops are commonly used to iterate an number of times, which is useful when the number of iterations depends on a given condition.Python has both of these loops and in this tutorial, you’ll learn about . In Python, you’ll generally use loops when you need to repeat a series of tasks an unknown number of times.Python loops are compound statements with a and a that runs until a given condition becomes false. The basic syntax of a loop is shown below:In this syntax, is an expression that the loop evaluates for its truth value. If the condition is true, then the loop body runs. Otherwise, the loop terminates. Note that the loop body can consist of one or more statements that must be indented properly.Here’s a more detailed breakdown of this syntax: is the keyword that initiates the loop header. is an expression evaluated for truthiness that defines the exit condition. consists of one or more statements to execute in each iteration.Here’s a quick example of how you can use a loop to iterate over a decreasing sequence of numbers:In this example, is the loop condition. If this condition returns a false value, the loop terminates. The body consists of a call to that displays the value on the screen. Next, you decrease the value of . This change will produce a different result when the loop evaluates the condition in the next iteration.The loop runs while the condition remains true. When the condition turns false, the loop terminates, and the program execution proceeds to the first statement after the loop body. In this example, the loop terminates when reaches a value less than or equal to .If the loop condition doesn’t become false, then you have a potentially infinite loop. Consider the following loop, and keep your fingers near the key combination to terminate its execution:In this example, the loop condition is . This condition works when you decrease by . However, if you decrease it by 2, the condition may never become false, resulting in a potentially infinite loop. In such cases, you can usually terminate the loop by pressing , which raises a exception on most operating systems.Note that the loop checks its condition first before anything else happens. If it’s false to start with, then the loop body will never run:

item.title: Building a Distributed E-commerce App with Go and gRPC – Part 1
item.author: Lakhan Samani
item.content:encoded:
Introduction & Project Overview
🚀 Why Build a Distributed System with Go & gRPC? Modern applications need to be scalable, fast, and efficient. A monolithic approach may work initially, but as your app grows, it becomes hard to maintain. This is where microservices come in!In this series, we’ll build a simple distributed e-commerce system using Go, gRPC, PostgreSQL, and Kubernetes. By the end of this series, you’ll understand how to:✅ Design gRPC APIs for communication between services.✅ Implement services with Go and handle user authentication.✅ Use middleware for logging, monitoring, and security.✅ Deploy services to Kubernetes and scale them dynamically.
📌 Project Overview: What Are We Building? We’ll build a simplified e-commerce backend with two core services:User Service 🧑‍💻 - Create users - Authenticate users with JWT - Retrieve user detailsOrder Service 📦 - Place an order (linked to a user)Communication between servicesDatabase for users & ordersPrometheus & AlertmanagerSetting up alerts for failuresDeployment & auto-scalingHere’s how the services interact:The User Service stores user data in PostgreSQL.The Order Service processes orders and links them to users.Both services communicate via gRPC instead of REST for high performance.
📝 Step 1: Setting Up the Project Make sure you have Go installed (v1.23+ recommended). Then install proto buff:go google.golang.org/protobuf/cmd/protoc-gen-go@latest go google.golang.org/grpc/cmd/protoc-gen-go-grpc@latest Now add them to your :go GOPATH
2️⃣ Create the Project Structure Run the following to set up the project:Note: please replace github.com/lakhansamani with your github or go package URL.ecom-grpc ecom-grpc apis userd orderd apis go mod init github.com/lakhansamani/ecom-grpc-apis .. userd go mod init github.com/lakhansamani/ecom-grpc-userd .. orderd go mod init github.com/lakhansamani/ecom-grpc-orderd 🎯 Next Steps In Part 2, we’ll define our gRPC APIs using Protobuf and implement the User Service in Go. Stay tuned! 🚀
